title: Problem

content.problemStatement:
Given a sorted array of **distinct** integers AAA and a target value KKK, return the index if the target is found. If not, return the index where it would be if inserted in order.

Complete the function and use Binary Search to solve this Problem.

Video Explanation
**Note:** Do not write anything outside the function.


Input Format

The first line of input contains two space-separated integers NNN and QQQ  denoting the number of elements in the array AAA and Q queries to search the given target element.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers denoting the elements in the array AAA.
The next QQQ lines contain the elements for which we need to find the search index positions


Output Format

For each query Q: Output position of KKK or the position where KKK is to be inserted.
constraints: All elements in A are distinct and sorted in ascending order.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains two space-separated integers N and Q, the size of the sorted array and the number of queries. The second line contains N distinct integers in strictly increasing order. Each of the next Q lines contains a single integer K, the target value whose position (or insertion index) must be reported. All indices are 0‑based.
content.outputFormat: For each query, output a single integer on its own line – the index of K if it exists in the array, otherwise the index where K would be inserted to keep the array sorted.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The array is sorted. The value 7 exists at index 3 (0‑based), so the answer is 3.
9 is not present. It would be placed between 7 (index 3) and 10 (index 4), therefore the insertion index is 4.
First query: 5 is at index 2. Second query: 2 is not present; it would be inserted before 3, i.e., at index 1.

solution.approach: Because the array is sorted and contains distinct elements, binary search can locate the target in O(log N) time. While searching, if the target is found we return its index. If the search finishes without finding the target, the left pointer points to the position where the target should be inserted to maintain sorted order. This gives the required insertion index. We repeat the process for each query.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log N) per query, O(Q log N) per test case, O(totalQueries log N) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{private static int searchInsert(int[] arr,int k){int left=0,right=arr.length-1;while(left<=right){int mid=left+(right-left)/2;if(arr[mid]==k)return mid;else if(arr[mid]>k)right=mid-1;else left=mid+1;}return left;}public static void main(String[] args)throws Exception{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- >0){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int N=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int Q=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int[] arr=new int[N];st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());for(int i=0;i<N;i++)arr[i]=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());for(int i=0;i<Q;i++){int k=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());sb.append(searchInsert(arr,k)).append('\n');}}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}
